topic: North Magnetic Pole deviation >18° from 120°E longitude
our_argument_summary: Our review says WMM2025 (released December 2024) published the pole at ~139.3°E over 14 months before PROS-006 was registered (2026-03-06). WIN-036 is logically entailed by WIN-007, which already confirms the same pole position. The dome extrapolates NOAA trajectory data with no dome-derived mechanism. Five batch-registered WINs (035-039) all filed and confirmed in the same week. Verdict: 'Std Model Explains' — non-discriminating.

relevant_files: WIN-007 entry in wins.json — monitor.py Domain #11 fetches NOAA NP.xy, checks lon > 130°E (per WIN-007 detail_evidence)
inject_ai_layer.py — W010 static confirmed entry (referenced in Cycle 1 review)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-036 maps to prediction W010. monitor.py's Domain #11 (NMP Longitude) fetches NOAA NP.xy data and checks if longitude > 130°E — but this test validates WIN-007, not WIN-036's specific >18° from 120°E threshold (>138°E). WIN-036 has no dedicated monitoring domain. Confirmation was manual comparison of WMM2025 output (139.298°E) against 120°E + 18° = 138°E.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Hardcoded. No code dynamically computes deviation from 120°E or tests against the 18° threshold. The NMP Longitude domain's lon > 130°E test serves WIN-007. WIN-036 was confirmed manually and recorded as a static entry.
data_sources_used: NOAA NP.xy (live time series, fetched by monitor.py for WIN-007)
NOAA/BGS WMM2025 (manual confirmation source, published December 2024)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None identified for WIN-036 specifically. The threshold of >138°E was already exceeded at registration time.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. Our review does not make specific code claims for WIN-036 beyond the NOAA trajectory extrapolation point, which is accurate.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: monitor.py's Domain #11 (NMP Longitude) fetches NOAA NP.xy and checks lon > 130°E, but this serves WIN-007 not WIN-036. WIN-036 (W010) has no dedicated monitoring domain. Confirmed manually from WMM2025 and stored as a static entry via inject_ai_layer.py.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The North Magnetic Pole position is tracked by WMM/IGRF/Swarm. WMM2025 published pole at ~139.3°E in Dec 2024. The dome relabels the cause as 'aetheric vortex threshold crossing' instead of core-mantle boundary flux lobe dynamics (Livermore et al. 2020, Nature Geoscience). No numerical prediction differs from WMM2025.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: WMM2025 released December 2024 with pole at ~139.3°E. PROS-006 registered 2026-03-06 — 14+ months later. Confirmed 2026-03-12 (6-day turnaround). Data was publicly available for over a year before 'prediction' was registered.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The 120°E reference meridian is a geographic coordinate. The 18° threshold has no derivation from dome parameters (disc_radius=20,015 km, firmament_height=9,086 km). The dome site explicitly says the prediction is 'consistent with the post-1990 exponential trajectory' — i.e., NOAA data extrapolation, not dome equations.

holes_found.description: Inconsistent code_analysis tags between WIN-007 and WIN-036. WIN-007 has relabels_standard=false while WIN-036 has relabels_standard=true, yet they describe the same measurement (pole at 139.3°E). WIN-007's detail_evidence explicitly says standard geophysics provides the 'complete causal mechanism' via Livermore et al. (2020) flux lobe dynamics — which is the definition of relabels_standard=true. The dome relabels this as 'aetheric threshold crossing' in both WINs. If WIN-036 relabels standard physics, WIN-007 does too.
holes_found.recommendation: Flag WIN-007's relabels_standard=false as inconsistent with its own evidence text. Either both should be true (standard physics explains pole position and acceleration) or both false. Given WIN-007's detail says 'fully explained by standard flux lobe dynamics,' relabels_standard should be true for both.

holes_found.description: The detail_claim still says 'unprecedented and predictive' which is our paraphrase, not the dome's actual wording. The dome (PROS-006) says: 'The North Magnetic Pole would be more than 18 deg east of the 120 deg E meridian, consistent with the post-1990 exponential trajectory.' The dome frames this as trajectory confirmation, not as unprecedented. This is a small strawman risk.
holes_found.recommendation: Adjust detail_claim to something like: 'Magnetic pole deviation exceeds 18° east of the 120°E meridian, confirming post-1990 exponential trajectory.' This matches the dome's actual framing and lets the rebuttal address the real claim.

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence mentions 'part of a batch of five predictions all registered the same day' but does not name the other four WINs. A reader cannot verify or follow up on the batch registration claim without cross-referencing.
holes_found.recommendation: Add the specific WINs: 'part of a batch of five predictions (WIN-035 through WIN-039) all registered on 6 March 2026 and confirmed within days.'

stronger_arguments.description: The 18° threshold was already exceeded at registration time — making this a zero-risk 'prediction.' At PROS-006 registration (2026-03-06), WMM2025 had already published the pole at 139.298°E (deviation = 19.3° from 120°E). Setting a threshold of 18° when the actual value is 19.3° guarantees success. This is equivalent to 'predicting' that yesterday's temperature exceeded 70°F by reading yesterday's weather report. Our text mentions the timeline but doesn't explicitly call out that the threshold was already exceeded at registration time.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model correctly identifies a real, scientifically significant phenomenon: the NMP's dramatic eastward acceleration since ~1990. The pole has crossed significant meridians at rates that required an emergency out-of-cycle WMM update in 2019. Tracking this and noting it exceeds historical reference points (like 18° from 120°E) reflects genuine engagement with geomagnetic data.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Recognizing a published phenomenon is not predicting it. WMM2025 published the pole at 139.3°E over a year before PROS-006 was registered. WIN-036 adds zero information beyond WIN-007 (which already places the pole at 139.3°E — arithmetic gives 19.3° from 120°E). The 18° threshold is not derived from dome geometry, was already exceeded at registration, and the dome predicts no different pole position than WMM2025. This is score inflation via tautological rephrasing.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 2 assessment: WIN-036's text has been substantially improved since Cycle 1. The key Cycle 1 recommendations — flagging the WIN-007 entailment, noting batch registration, and adding the registration/confirmation timeline — have all been incorporated into the current detail_evidence. The argument is now significantly stronger.

Remaining issues are lower severity:

1. **WIN-007 tag inconsistency (moderate)**: The most substantive finding this cycle is the relabels_standard tag mismatch between WIN-007 (false) and WIN-036 (true). These WINs describe the same data point. WIN-007's own text says the phenomenon is 'fully explained by standard flux lobe dynamics (Livermore 2020)' — which is literally the definition of relabels_standard. This inconsistency could confuse the decider pipeline or produce incorrect aggregate counts in Part 4.6.

2. **Detail_claim framing (minor)**: 'Unprecedented and predictive' is our editorial gloss, not the dome's claim. The dome says 'consistent with the post-1990 exponential trajectory.' Since we pride ourselves on not strawmanning, we should match their actual language.

3. **Zero-risk threshold (not yet in text)**: Our detail_evidence notes the WMM2025 timeline but doesn't explicitly state the punchline: the 18° threshold was already exceeded at PROS-006 registration. Adding one sentence — 'The threshold was already exceeded by the data used to register the prediction' — would make the post-hoc nature unmistakable.

**Dome defender's strongest counter**: 'The 18° threshold was derived from our exponential trajectory model, not picked from WMM2025. We registered it as a formal milestone crossing, not a new prediction.'

**Rebuttal**: Even granting the 'milestone' framing, the dome's exponential trajectory was itself fitted to NOAA NP.xy data (post-hoc). WMM2025 makes equivalent trajectory forecasts with published uncertainty bounds. And counting a milestone crossing as a separate WIN when WIN-007 already confirms the underlying pole position is score inflation regardless of how you frame it.

Overall: the verdict holds solidly. Text improvements from Cycle 1 have addressed the major gaps. The remaining issues are a cross-WIN tag inconsistency and two minor framing points.

citations_verified: WIN-007 cross-reference (#win007) — verified WIN-007 exists with pole at 139.3°E, link format correct
citations_unchecked: WMM2025 'December 2024' release date — consistent with NOAA's 5-year update cycle (WMM2020 released Dec 2019) but exact date not independently verified this cycle
